title: Studies with babies
text: Language is the most complex communicative system known. Yet, most humans find it surprisingly easy to acquire language. What mechanisms support this readiness for language acquisition? This is one of the main research questions at the NeuroDevComp group, and neonates are key to investigate it, since they still have not received extensive experience with language yet! To investigate this issue, we use a techique called fNIRS, in which we measure newborns’ brain spontaneous activity in response to different parts types of speech.

title: Studies with babies
text: Language is the most complex communicative system known. Yet, most humans find it surprisingly easy to acquire language. What mechanisms support this readiness for language acquisition? title: Studies with babies
text:
Infants

Our group research focuses on the early acquisition of language. We are interested in how babies can detect and process different parts of speech. Infants are known to pick up rules and habits of their language’s environment very quickly. We are interested in the processing and segmentation of speech into small units like syllables. To study this, we use different techniques: behavioral ones, in which we take parameters such as the baby looking time and reaction to what they are listening to. We also use other techniques like eye-tracking (a technique used to track the infant’s gaze towards a target), or fNIRS. In all experiments parents are at all times with their baby. This part of the project includes babies from 4 to 11 months old, and different linguistic environments are welcome!

title: Neurodevelopment and Comparative Cognition
text:
Neurodevelopment and Comparative Cognition
















The Neurodevelopment and Comparative Cognition (NeuroDevComp) research group at the Institut de Recerca Sant Joan de Déu (Barcelona, Spain), led by Dr. Chiara Santolin, focuses on understanding the mechanisms underlying cognitive and neurodevelopmental processes across species. By combining comparative and developmental approaches, the group investigates how early-life experiences shape brain development and cognitive functions. Their interdisciplinary work bridges psychology, neuroscience, and biology to uncover insights into typical development, aiming to inform language acquisition and language evolution.

title: People
text:
I am a cognitive scientist specializing in language acquisition and comparative cognition. I serve as a Principal Investigator at the Institut de Recerca Sant Joan de Déu. My research explores the cognitive mechanisms behind language learning, particularly how infants detect and categorize linguistic structures from an early age. I am also interested in comparative cognition, investigating whether similar learning processes exist in non-human species. Through my work, I aim to understand the evolutionary roots of language and the fundamental principles guiding learning across species.
I earned my Ph.D. in Psychology from the University of Padova in 2016, following a Master’s degree in Experimental Psychology and Cognitive Science from the same institution in 2013. After completing my doctorate, I pursued postdoctoral research at the University of Wisconsin–Madison’s Waisman Center, focusing on infant language learning. In 2017, I joined the Speech Acquisition and Perception Group at Universitat Pompeu Fabra in Barcelona, where I investigated the acquisition of syntactic and syllabic structures, categorization processes, and infants’ preferences for speech.
In 2024, I was awarded an ERC Starting Grant for my project Gates to Language (GALA), which aims to explore the evolutionary patterns of mechanisms involved in language learning across humans and non-human animals. Currently, continuing my research on statistical learning, infant language development, and animal cognition.

title: People
text:
Post-doctoral researchers


Gonzalo García-Castro                     


I am a post-doctoral researcher investigating language acquisition and how young minds make sense of the world. My work focuses on developmental psychology and psycholinguistics, studying how humans process language at different stages in development: from newborns to infants, toddlers, and adults. Under the GALA project, I investigate how neonates, infants, and non-human animals process different syllabic structures, searching for common mechanisms supporting language processing. To do this, I use a combination of eye-tracking, functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), and computational modelling. with a special focus on computational reproducibility. Bayesian statistics are a big part of how I try to make sense of data.
In 2024 I obtained my PhD at the Center for Brain and Cognition, Universitat Pompeu Fabra, supervised by Prof. Núria Sebastian-Galles. I earned my BSc in Psychology from the Universidad de Oviedo in 2013, and my MSc in Neurosciences at the Universitat de Barcelona in 2018.

title: People
text:
PhD students


Flora Chartier       


I am a PhD student at Universitat Pompeu Fabra in Barcelona, enrolled in the PhD Program in Biomedicine. I am interested in the early acquisition and processing of language by infants, using different techniques such as behavioral ones, eye-tracking and fNIRS.
I obtained my MSc in Integrative Biology and Physiology, specialized in Behavioral Neurosciences in La Sorbonne Université and spent a year working as a research engineer on the early acquisition of language in the INCC–Language & Cognition team in Paris. I am currently involved in the GALA project investigating how babies, at only a few months old, perceive syllables.

title: People
text:
Staff

Laura S. Piracún-García       


I work as research assistant for the GALA project. I facilitate research by aiding data collection, providing logistic and administrative support, and taking care of the call center to invite families to participate in our studies. I obtained my MSc in International Experimental and Clinical Linguistics at the University of Potsdam (Potsdam, Germany), and my BA in Psychology at Bethany College (Kansas, USA). I am very interested in research aiming to understand language acquisition mechanisms and strategies, with a special focus on understudied populations, languages, and dialects.

title: Animal research
text: Did you know that not only humans are able to communicate and process language? In our research group we are interested in using a comparative approach to understand the acquisition and processing of language. For this, not only are we stuying humans but also non-human animals, like Rattus Norvegicus, to test their speech processing abilities, and in particular, syllables. With our research, we hope to be able to understand at a closer level the processing of the smallest units that make up language.
